agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Integration of Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) bounded contexts to enable stock-aware order placement. Currently orders are placed without stock validation — the request asks for real-time stock checking at order creation time and a live stock indicator for the sales team.",
    "bounded_context": "Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. This is an anti-corruption layer concern — neither context should directly depend on the other's internals.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "StockLevel", "definition": "Warehouse context: the current on-hand quantity of a Product in the warehouse. Integer >= 0. Authoritative source of physical inventory truth." },
      { "term": "Product", "definition": "Warehouse context: a stockable item tracked by the warehouse. Identified by a Product ID shared across contexts." },
      { "term": "Order", "definition": "Sales context: a customer's intent to purchase one or more products. Contains OrderLines." },
      { "term": "OrderLine", "definition": "Sales context: a single line item on an Order, referencing a Product ID and a desired Quantity." },
      { "term": "Quantity", "definition": "Sales context: the number of units of a product requested by the customer on an OrderLine." },
      { "term": "Stock Reservation", "definition": "PROPOSED: a temporary claim against StockLevel made when an Order is accepted, preventing overselling. This term does not yet exist in either context." },
      { "term": "Stock Available Indicator", "definition": "PROPOSED: a read-model projection of StockLevel visible to the Sales context. Eventually consistent — NOT the authoritative StockLevel." },
      { "term": "Blocked Order", "definition": "PROPOSED: an Order that has been rejected or held because insufficient stock exists to fulfill it." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "StockLevel for any Product must never go below 0.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This represents a physical constraint — you cannot ship goods you do not have. Violating this corrupts inventory data and leads to unfulfillable commitments.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any sequence of stock decrements and increments, assert StockLevel >= 0 at all times."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The Warehouse context is the sole authority on current StockLevel. The Sales context must never own, mutate, or authoritatively store stock quantities.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Two sources of truth for inventory is a data integrity violation. If Sales maintains its own stock count, divergence is inevitable and leads to overselling or phantom stock.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that no write to StockLevel originates from the Sales bounded context."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "An Order must not be confirmed if the sum of requested Quantity across its OrderLines exceeds available (unreserved) StockLevel for any referenced Product.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy — the business could choose to allow backorders, partial fulfillment, or override for VIP customers. A VP could change this rule tomorrow.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given Product X with StockLevel 5 and 3 already reserved, when an Order requests Quantity 4 of Product X, then the Order is blocked."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Stock checking at order time must account for reservations (stock already claimed by other accepted-but-not-yet-picked orders), not just raw StockLevel.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without reservation accounting, two concurrent orders can each see StockLevel = 5 and each claim 5 units, resulting in StockLevel going negative — violating BR-1.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given StockLevel 10 and existing reservations totaling 7, available stock is 3. An order for Quantity 4 must be blocked."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The 'stock available' indicator shown to the Sales team is eventually consistent — it is a read projection, not a transactional guarantee. It must never be used as the authoritative stock check for order acceptance.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the read-model indicator is treated as authoritative, the system will oversell during high concurrency. The indicator is a UX convenience; the actual stock check must happen transactionally at order acceptance time.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the order acceptance/blocking decision queries the Warehouse context (or its reservation system) directly, not the Sales-side read model."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Reservations must have a time-to-live (TTL). Unredeemed reservations expire and release stock back to the available pool.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The TTL duration is a business policy (could be 15 minutes, 24 hours, etc.), but the concept of expiry is necessary to prevent indefinite stock lockup from abandoned orders.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a reservation created at T, when TTL elapses without fulfillment, then StockLevel available increases by the reserved Quantity."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel 10 and 0 existing reservations in Warehouse context",
      "when": "A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for Quantity 5 of that Product",
      "then": "The Order is accepted, and a Stock Reservation of 5 is created in the Warehouse context, reducing available stock to 5"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel 10 and existing reservations totaling 8",
      "when": "A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for Quantity 3 of that Product",
      "then": "The Order is blocked, the customer is informed of insufficient stock, and no reservation is created"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel 10 and existing reservations totaling 8",
      "when": "A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for Quantity 2 of that Product",
      "then": "The Order is accepted and a reservation of 2 is created, reducing available stock to 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "Two customers simultaneously attempt to order the last 5 units of a Product (StockLevel 5, 0 reservations)",
      "when": "Both orders are submitted at nearly the same time",
      "then": "Exactly one order is accepted and one is blocked. StockLevel never goes below 0. No double-reservation occurs."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel 20",
      "when": "The Sales team views the product listing",
      "then": "A 'stock available' indicator reflects approximate current availability, updated within the acceptable staleness window (defined by the business, e.g., 5 seconds)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "An Order was accepted and stock reserved, but the Order is subsequently cancelled before picking",
      "when": "The cancellation is processed",
      "then": "The reservation is released, and available stock increases by the reserved Quantity"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "An Order with multiple OrderLines referencing Products A (Qty 3) and B (Qty 2)",
      "when": "Product A has sufficient stock but Product B does not",
      "then": "The entire Order is blocked — no partial reservation is made. Stock for Product A is not reserved."
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Order placed for a Product that exists in Sales but has no record in Warehouse (new product, data sync lag)", "expected_behavior": "Order must be blocked. Absence of stock data is not 'unlimited stock' — it is 'unknown stock'. System should treat unknown as zero or raise an explicit integration error." },
    { "scenario": "Stock received from supplier (StockLevel increases) while a previously-blocked order is pending", "expected_behavior": "The system should NOT auto-retry blocked orders unless the business explicitly wants a backorder queue. Blocked means blocked — the customer must re-place the order. (This is a policy decision that needs business input.)" },
    { "scenario": "Reservation TTL expires on an order that the customer believes is confirmed", "expected_behavior": "The customer must be notified that their order could not be held. The order transitions to a cancelled or needs-reconfirmation state. Silent expiry is unacceptable." },
    { "scenario": "Warehouse context is temporarily unavailable (network partition, downtime)", "expected_behavior": "Sales context must fail-closed: orders cannot be accepted if stock cannot be verified. Accepting orders blindly during outages recreates the original problem of orders without stock checks." },
    { "scenario": "Bulk order from a B2B customer requesting 10,000 units where StockLevel is 10,000 exactly", "expected_behavior": "Accepting this order would reserve ALL stock, blocking every other customer. Business must decide: should there be a maximum reservation percentage per order, or is first-come-first-served acceptable?" },
    { "scenario": "StockLevel is updated (e.g., inventory correction reduces it) below the sum of existing reservations", "expected_behavior": "System must detect over-reservation and trigger a reconciliation process — some reservations may need to be revoked. This requires a defined priority scheme (FIFO, customer tier, etc.)." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Prevents overselling, which directly reduces refund costs, compensation payouts, and emergency procurement at premium prices. However, overly aggressive blocking may reduce conversion rates — orders that could have been fulfilled from incoming supply will be rejected. The reservation TTL policy directly affects revenue: too short loses sales, too long locks up inventory.",
    "operational": "Introduces a new cross-context dependency that increases system complexity. The Warehouse context now has a new concept (Reservations) that must be managed, expired, and reconciled. Operations teams need monitoring for reservation leaks, stock discrepancies, and integration lag. The 'stock available' indicator requires a new read-model with its own infrastructure (event subscription, projection, staleness monitoring).",
    "user": "Sales team gains real-time visibility into stock, reducing time spent on manual stock checks and customer callbacks. Customers get immediate feedback on order viability instead of delayed rejection emails. However, eventual consistency means the indicator may show 'available' when stock has just been reserved — leading to frustration when the order is then blocked at submission.",
    "risk": "HIGH — This is a cross-bounded-context integration that creates tight coupling if done wrong. The greatest risk is treating the stock indicator as authoritative (bypassing transactional reservation), which would cause overselling under concurrency. Secondary risk: the Warehouse context becomes a single point of failure for order placement if synchronous calls are used without circuit-breaking.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Orders that previously would have been accepted (and failed later) are now blocked upfront. Better experience overall, but some customers may see 'out of stock' due to indicator staleness even when stock exists." },
      { "group": "Sales Team", "impact": "Gains a stock indicator in their UI. Must understand it is approximate — they cannot promise customers exact quantities based on the indicator alone." },
      { "group": "Warehouse Operations", "impact": "Must now manage Reservations as a first-class concept. Reservation expiry, reconciliation after inventory corrections, and integration monitoring are new operational responsibilities." },
      { "group": "Engineering / Platform", "impact": "Must build and maintain an anti-corruption layer, event-driven read model, and reservation system. Ongoing cost of monitoring cross-context integration health." },
      { "group": "Finance / Accounting", "impact": "Reserved-but-not-yet-fulfilled stock may affect inventory valuation and reporting. Need clarity on whether reserved stock counts as 'committed' in financial reports." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The feature request contains a fundamental contradiction and several critical ambiguities. It asks to 'automatically block' orders (implying synchronous, transactional stock validation) AND show 'stock available in real time' (implying an eventually-consistent read model) — but does not clarify the relationship between these two mechanisms. It also does not specify: reservation semantics, TTL policy, partial fulfillment rules, backorder behavior, concurrency strategy, or what 'block' means (reject permanently? hold for retry?). Multiple domain concepts are implied but undefined.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "StockLevel >= 0 is an inviolable invariant",
      "Warehouse context must remain the sole authority on stock",
      "A reservation mechanism is required to prevent overselling under concurrency",
      "The read-model indicator must not be used as the transactional stock check"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether 'block' means permanent rejection or a hold/backorder state",
      "Whether partial fulfillment of multi-line orders is acceptable",
      "Reservation TTL duration and expiry behavior",
      "Whether blocked orders should auto-retry when stock is replenished",
      "Acceptable staleness window for the stock indicator",
      "Whether bulk orders should have reservation caps"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The integration should use an anti-corruption layer, not direct context coupling",
      "The stock indicator is eventually consistent and not transactional",
      "Reservations are a new concept that currently exists in neither context",
      "All OrderLines in an Order must be fulfillable for the Order to be accepted (all-or-nothing)",
      "The system should fail-closed when the Warehouse context is unavailable"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The feature request implies two conflicting integration styles: (1) 'automatically block if stock is insufficient' requires synchronous, transactional stock validation at order time — the Sales context must call into or through the Warehouse context. (2) 'stock available indicator in real time' implies an eventually-consistent read model. These are fundamentally different mechanisms with different consistency guarantees. If only the read model is built, blocking will be unreliable. If only synchronous validation is built, the indicator adds latency. The request does not clarify whether these are two separate features or one unified mechanism.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Sales Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Build both — a transactional reservation system for order blocking AND a separate eventually-consistent read model for the indicator. Two integration points, higher complexity, correct semantics.",
        "B: Build only the transactional reservation system, and derive the indicator from reservation data. Simpler, but indicator staleness depends on reservation system design.",
        "C: Build only the read model and use it for both display and blocking. Simpler, but will oversell under concurrency — violates BR-1."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A. The two use cases have fundamentally different consistency requirements. Conflating them guarantees either overselling (Option C) or poor UX (forcing transactional reads for a display indicator). Accept the complexity."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The term 'block' is undefined. Does blocking mean: (a) the order is permanently rejected and the customer must re-submit, (b) the order is placed in a 'pending stock' hold state and auto-confirmed when stock arrives (backorder), or (c) the order is held for manual review by the sales team? Each interpretation has radically different domain model implications, UX, and operational cost.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Warehouse Operations"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject — order is refused, customer must retry later",
        "B: Backorder — order is queued and auto-fulfilled when stock arrives",
        "C: Hold — order enters a review state for sales team intervention"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Clarify with the product owner. Recommend starting with Option A (reject) as the simplest to implement correctly, with Option B as a future enhancement."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No specification for multi-line order behavior when only some products have sufficient stock. Should the entire order be blocked (all-or-nothing), or should partial fulfillment be allowed (accept available lines, block others)?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Finance"],
      "options": [
        "A: All-or-nothing — entire order blocked if any line is insufficient",
        "B: Partial fulfillment — accept lines with stock, block or split the rest"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Default to all-or-nothing (Option A) as it is simpler and avoids the complexity of split orders, partial shipments, and split invoicing. Escalate to product owner for confirmation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No reservation lifecycle is defined. Stock Reservations are essential to prevent overselling, but the spec provides no guidance on: reservation TTL, what happens on order cancellation, how inventory corrections interact with existing reservations, or reservation priority when stock is scarce.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Warehouse Operations", "Engineering", "Finance"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define a full Reservation aggregate in the Warehouse context with TTL, expiry events, and reconciliation rules",
        "B: Use a simpler 'soft lock' mechanism without formal reservation lifecycle"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Without formal reservations, overselling under concurrency is inevitable. The TTL and lifecycle details must be specified by the business before implementation."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
